mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz GLC63 AMG engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned GLC63 AMG engine typically costs £6,500–£10,000 supply only, with the GLC63 S variant at the higher end. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 depending on the specialist. A full engine replacement on a Mercedes can range from £9,000 to £13,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your GLC‑Class.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz GLC63 AMG engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M177 is considered one of AMG's most celebrated modern V8s. However, it has well‑documented vulnerabilities. The most critical is oil separator failure, which often leads to rear main seal leaks. Ignition coil failure is also common, with coils failing at as little as 40,000 miles. Intercooler coolant leaks are another known issue. Timing chain wear and carbon buildup are also concerns. The M177 is sensitive to neglected maintenance. With proper maintenance and proactive attention to known issues, the M177 can be a reliable engine — but it requires deep pockets and careful ownership.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz GLC63 AMG?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issues are oil separator failure (leading to rear main seal leaks — whistling noise, oil leaks, unstable idle), ignition coil failure (misfires, rough running), intercooler coolant leaks (coolant loss, misfires), timing chain wear (rattling on cold start), oil leaks from valve cover gaskets and timing covers, and carbon buildup on intake valves (rough idle, poor fuel economy).

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a GLC63 AMG engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the vehicle value and condition. Against a well‑kept GLC63 AMG worth £35,000–£60,000+, a £7,000–£9,000 recon is 15–20% of the car's value — often worth it for a cherished example. The GLC63 AMG is a desirable performance SUV, and engine replacement is a significant but justifiable investment for a well‑maintained example.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz GLC63 AMG engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is M177.980, found on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation. It's the 4.0‑litre twin‑turbo V8 fitted to the GLC63 AMG X253/C253 from 2017–2022. The engine is handcrafted by AMG in Affalterbach. The GLC63 produces 476 hp; the GLC63 S produces 503 hp.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M177 oil separator, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The oil separator must be replaced if it's failing — it's a critical component of the crankcase ventilation system. A failing oil separator can cause excessive crankcase pressure or vacuum, leading to rear main seal failure and other oil leaks. A preventative replacement costs £1,500–£4,000. Ignoring it can lead to $10K+ in engine repairs. At the first sign of whistling noises, unstable idle, or oil leaks, book an inspection immediately.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: GLC‑Class GLC63 AMG — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance — catch before rear main seal fails

rows.problem: Rear main seal failure (from oil separator)
rows.whenItMakesSense: Labour‑intensive but essential — prevents oil loss
Replace all eight coils — prevent misfire damage
Catch it early — before coolant damages the engine
Essential maintenance — catch before catastrophic failure
Piston‑valve contact means little is left to repair
Replacement cost justified on high‑value examples

buyingChecks.title: Buying This GLC63 AMG? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (critical for M177 longevity)
* No oil leaks from the rear of the engine (oil separator and rear main seal health)
* No misfires or rough running (ignition coil health)
* No coolant loss or sweet smells (intercooler coolant leak warning)
* Still under factory or extended warranty — GLC63 repairs are expensive
avoidIf: * Oil leaks from the rear of the engine — oil separator or rear main seal may be failing
* Misfires or rough running — ignition coils may be failing
* Unexplained coolant loss — intercooler coolant leak may be developing
* Cold‑start rattling and no timing chain history — budget for chain work
* No service history — the M177 is unforgiving of neglect
* Any history of engine failure or major repairs without documentation

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The GLC63 AMG is Mercedes‑Benz's fire‑breathing V8 SUV — a 4.0‑litre twin‑turbo M177 engine producing up to 503 hp and 700 Nm torque. It's one of AMG's most celebrated engines, delivering serious power and performance. However, as these vehicles accumulate mileage, several expensive problems can emerge. The most critical is oil separator failure, which often leads to rear main seal leaks — a repair that can cost thousands. Ignition coil failure is also common, with coils failing at as little as 40,000 miles. Intercooler coolant leaks are another known issue that can cause catastrophic engine damage if ignored. The M177 is sensitive to neglected maintenance. With typical GLC63 values ranging from £35,000 to £60,000+, an engine replacement is a major financial commitment — a well‑maintained example with full service history and an active warranty is the safest choice.

cta.label: → Compare GLC63 AMG engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz GLC‑Class GLC63 AMG (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Oil Leaks — Check for oil around the transmission bellhousing and rear of the engine (oil separator / rear main seal warning) — see the map above
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clattering (timing chain tensioner warning)
* Misfires — Check for smooth idle; any misfires may indicate ignition coil failure
* Coolant System — Check for sweet smells, unexplained coolant loss (intercooler coolant leak warning)
* Service History — Including regular oil changes (critical for M177 longevity)
* Ignition Coils — Check for any history of coil replacement (common failure)
* Warranty Status — Check if the vehicle still has a transferable warranty — GLC63 repairs are expensive
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate ignition, timing, oil separator or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
